coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent hydrogels based on oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose with excellent adsorption and sensing abilities for Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: He, Xiaoyan
Jia, Hui
Sun, Nan
Hou, Miaomiao
Tan, Zheping
Lu, Xiaoquan
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal contamination in water and soil are harmful and destructive to the environment, it has always been regarded as a big problem. Herein, we developed a self-healing fluorescent hydrogel based on oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose with excellent sensing and adsorption abilities for Ag+. The detection and adsorption effects of hydrogels on heavy metal ions were studied. It turned out that the fluorescent hydrogel has sensitive detection and high adsorption capacity for Ag+, the detection limit was 3.798 μM, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 407 mg/g. The adsorption isotherm fitted the Langmuir model well, and the pseudo-secondary model for adsorption kinetics fitted well. The hydrogel could heal itself without external stimulus, it could be easily regenerated 7 times without loss of adsorption performance. In short, the prepared hydrogel has capability of self-healing, detecting and adsorbing heavy metal ions at the same time, good mechanical strength, these all made it a promising long-life adsorbent and provided a new way for wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent hydrogel
Self-healing
Ag+ detection and adsorption
Oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Desorption and reusability study of hydrogels
Preparation of self-healing double network fluorescent hydrogel
Characterization of self-healing double network fluorescent hydrogel
Adsorption capacity to heavy metal ions
Mechanism of adsorption and fluorescence sensing of heavy metals

Special Fund Project for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Funds for Creative Research Groups of Gansu Province (No. 21JR7RA160 ), the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22174110 , 22127803 ), the Industrial Support Plan of Gansu Provincial Department of Education ( 2021cyzc-01 ) and Special Fund Project for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development by the Central Government (No. 2020-2060503-17 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface characterization of B-CDs. (a) Fluorescence spectrum (b) TEM images; (c) FTIR spectra.
The characterization of self-healing BCS hydrogel. (a)FTIR spectra; (b-c) XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s for self-healing BCS hydrogel; (d) SEM images.
TGA curves of B-CDs, self-healing BCS hydrogel and hydrogel without B-CDs.

Study on the Fluorescence Properties of self-healing BCS hydrogel. (a)Fluorescence spectra; (b) Effect of storage time on fluorescence intensity of self-healing BCS hydrogel; (c) Comparison of fluorescence (FL) intensity of self-healing BCS hydrogel before and after the addition of various metal ions(100 mM); (d) Comparison of FL intensity of self-healing BCS hydrogel before and after the addition of M + Ag(I) (M: one of competing metal ions).

(a-b) Fluorescence intensity of self-healing BCS hydrogel with different concentrations of Ag+ in H2O solution (0–400 μmol; pH: 5; quenching time: 0.5 h; temperature: 25 °C); (c) The fluorescence quenching of the self-healing BCS hydrogel with increasing Ag+ ion concentration.

Adsorption mechanistic investigations of self-healing BCS hydrogel for Ag+. (a) Adsorption progress of the self-healing BCS hydrogel; (b) The adsorption capacity for Ag+ at different pH; (c) Adsorption isotherms of Ag+; (d) Fitting curves of Langmuir isotherm model; (e) Time dependent of Ag+ on self-healing BCS hydrogel; (f)pseudo-second-order model for Ag+ absorption; (g)SEM-mapping images of self-healing BCS hydrogel after Ag+ adsorption.
Adsorption capacity of self-healing BCS hydrogel after regeneration.
FTIR and XPS analysis of self-healing BCS hydrogel before and after Ag+ adsorption. (a) FTIR analysis; (b) XPS survey of BCS hydrogel-Ag+; (c-e) Ag3d, O1s, N1sof BCS hydrogel-Ag+.
Mechanism diagram of hydrogel detection and adsorption of Ag ions.
Self-healing properties of BCS hydrogel. (a-b) Self-healing real photograph of self-healing BCS hydrogel; (c) Self-healing mechanism of self-healing BCS hydrogel.
The schematic of the preparation of the self-healing BCS hydrogel.
Comparison of the adsorption capacities of different adsorbents for Ag(I).

row.entry: MoS2 nanosheet initiated smart polymeric hydrogel

head.ce:title: Fluorescent hydrogels based on oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose with excellent adsorption and sensing abilities for Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal contamination in water and soil are harmful and destructive to the environment, it has always been regarded as a big problem. Herein, we developed a self-healing fluorescent hydrogel based on oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose with excellent sensing and adsorption abilities for Ag+. The detection and adsorption effects of hydrogels on heavy metal ions were studied. It turned out that the fluorescent hydrogel has sensitive detection and high adsorption capacity for Ag+, the detection limit was 3.798 μM, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 407 mg/g. The adsorption isotherm fitted the Langmuir model well, and the pseudo-secondary model for adsorption kinetics fitted well. The hydrogel could heal itself without external stimulus, it could be easily regenerated 7 times without loss of adsorption performance. In short, the prepared hydrogel has capability of self-healing, detecting and adsorbing heavy metal ions at the same time, good mechanical strength, these all made it a promising long-life adsorbent and provided a new way for wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, silver is widely used in agriculture, pharmaceuticals, mirrors or food industries because of its excellent electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity and antibacterial properties [1–3]. People always pay attention to its advantages but ignore its harmfulness to the environment. With the progress of society, toxic heavy metals has become one of the most serious issues threatening human safety and environmental stability [4–6]. Among these heavy metals, even at very low concentrations, Ag(I) exhibits higher toxicity than other heavy metals such as copper or mercury [7–9]. Due to the high toxicity of silver (I) to aquatic organisms and humans, it has become increasingly necessary to monitor this accumulation [10].

Carbon dots (CDs) have characters of high photo stability, low photo-bleaching, non-toxic, and biocompatible, enabling their promising usage as heavy metal sensor in the environmental, biological, and medical fields [11–14]. Notably, the luminescence properties of carbon dots can be improved via Heteroatom-doping and sensitive to different metal ions. The feature will pave the way toward fluorescence detection materials to be promising materials [15–17]. Besides this, hydrogels also have bright applications in removing of heavy metals from polluted water. Recently, the hydrogels based on carbon-based nanodots used for the detection of heavy metal ions had been reported largely. For example, Aula A Alwattaret al. synthesized an amphiphilic fluorescent hydrogel containing pyrene and perylene to detect and adsorb Cu2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ [18]. Zhang Dongdong et al. prepared the hydrogel of PNIPAAm-CD to detect and adsorb iron ions [19]. Li Meng had prepared a CDs-Rho-based cellulose fluorescent hydrogel that can detect and remove Hg2+ [20]. Lei Xia developed a new type of fluorescent nanocellulose hydrogel based on cellulose nanocrystals as the skeleton that can detect and adsorb mercury ions [21]. Although these reported adsorbents achieved the purpose of simultaneous detection and adsorption, they cannot simultaneously adsorb a large number of metal ions while achieving good selectivity. Furthermore, the hydrogel with self-healing properties could recover automatically without the influence of external conditions when it was damaged, greatly improving energy efficiency of the hydrogel [21–24]. However, self-healing hydrogels for heavy metal adsorption have rarely been reported.

In this work, self-healing fluorescent hydrogels with excellent adsorption and sensing abilities for Ag+ were firstly prepared by using oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose and dynamic Schiff-base bonds reaction. We added B-doped carbon dots into the hydrogel for the optimal detection and adsorption of Ag+. The fluorescent performance for Ag(I) was proved, the detection sensitivity and selectivity were also explored. Furthermore, equilibrium isotherms and adsorption kinetic models were used to explore the adsorption behavior of Ag(I) to this hydrogel. The mechanism of highly efficient adsorption and detection of Ag(I) for this novel hydrogel was explicated. The direct determination and fast adsorption of silver ion using fluorescent hydrogel was reported for the first time in this paper.

Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), l-ascorbic acid (AA), Sodium periodate, Boric acid (H3BO3), acrylamide (AM), Ammonium persulphate, N′,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA,99 %), N,N,N′,N′-Tetramethylethylenediamine, gelatin, all analytical reagents were used without further treatment.

Boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) were prepared by traditionally hydrothermal approach [25–27]. Briefly, 0.55 g AA and 0.25 gH3BO3 were dissolved in a mixed solution of 12.5 mL deionized water and 12.5 mL ethanol. Afterwards, the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 10. The solution was heated at 180 °C for 9 h, and then naturally cooled. The resulting solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitate.

Oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose was prepared using reported methods [28]. Adding CMC (6.0 g, 13.82 mmol) to distilled water to dissolve and add sodium periodate (2.50 g, 11.68 mmol) solution. After stirring for 24 h at room temperature in the dark, 5 mL of ethylene glycol was added and stirred for three hours and then the reaction was stopped. The mixture solution was dialyzed with distilled water (MWCO: 8–14 kDa) for three days, changed the water several times, and finally freeze-dried to obtain pure Oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose (oxi-CMC).

Self-healing fluorescent hydrogel was prepared by free radical polymerization and Schiff base reaction. Initially, gelatin (0.3 g) and oxi-CMC (0.3 g) were dissolved in 6 mL PBS buffer solution and heated at 80 °C for 30 min. Then, acrylamide (0.5 g) was dissolved in the above mixed solution under stirring, and MBA (0.005 g), carbon dots (0.025 g) were added, followed by sonication for 30 min. Subsequently, after nitrogen gas was added and purge for 60 min, APS (0.20 g) was added. Then the mixed solution was put at room temperature for 180 min to form a hydrogel. The prepared hydrogel was immersed in water to remove the residue, and the water was repeatedly replaced. Finally, freeze-dry the self-healing fluorescent hydrogel (self-healing BCS hydrogel) to prepare for characterization and adsorption experiments.

The self-healing fluorescent hydrogel was put in water samples with different concentrations of Ag+ ions. Then recording the fluorescence spectrum of the sample with a fluorescence spectrophotometer after shaking for 30 min. In order to conduct selective experiments, different competitive metal ion solutions (Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, K+) were added.

To explore the adsorption performance and behavior of Ag+ on self-healing fluorescent hydrogels, batches of adsorption experiments were designed, including different concentrations Ag+ solution (100–1000 mg/L) and different contact time (5–120 min) and pH (1–7). Adsorption experiment proceeded as follows: at 293 K, adding 15 mg of hydrogel to 20 mL of the prepared silver solution and placing it in a constant temperature shaker at 180 r/min for 2 h. After the adsorption process was stopped, a filter with a syringe was used to filter the Ag+ solution, and ICP-OES was used to further measure the concentration of the filtrate.

The adsorption capacity (Qe) and adsorption efficiency (E) of the hydrogel were described as follows:
(1)

In order to accurately study the influence of hydrogel contact time on the content of adsorbed silver, we used the pseudo-first-order model, pseudo-second-order model and intra-particle diffusion model of the adsorption kinetics to perform the fitting [29,30]. The formulas are as follows:Equation of pseudo-first-order kinetics model:
(3)

where k1 (min−1), k2 (g/(mg min)), and kp ((g mg−1 min−1)) were the rate constants for the three models, respectively. C was a constant representing the thickness dependence.

The adsorption isotherm was used to explore the adsorption type of Ag+ on the hydrogel adsorbent. In this work, four models were used to describe adsorption type.Langmuir model:
(6)
Q e
=
Q m
K L

K L

Freundlich model:
(7)

(9)

C e
Temkin isotherm model:
(10)

= B × ln
K T
C e
where Qe (mg/g) and Qm (mg/g) were the adsorption capacity at equilibrium and the maximum adsorption capacity, respectively. Ce (mg/mL) was the concentration of metal ions solution at equilibrium. KL (L/mg) was the Langmuir constant, which related to the free energy and affinity of adsorption. KF (L/mg) and n were Freundlich constants related to adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity, respectively. KDR (mol2/KJ2) and ε represented the average adsorption free energy and adsorption potential energy in the adsorption process in the D-R model, respectively. KT (L/mg) and B represented binding energy constant and adsorption heat in the Temkin model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption and reusability study of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the reusability function of the self-healing fluorescent hydrogel for removing heavy metal from wastewater, we performed cycling experiments on the hydrogel. 20 mL of 0.1 mol/L HCl was added to the metal ion-loaded hydrogel, stirred at 180 rpm for 3 h. The hydrogels were thoroughly washed with deionized water, dried and reused for the next adsorption process. The regeneration performance was performed 7 times by adsorption/desorption cycles according to the method described above, using the same heavy metal ion solution for each experiment.

The following equation calculated the regeneration rate:
(11)
The regeneration rate =
Q e
Q e
0
where Qe (mg/ g) was equilibrium adsorption capacity of hydrogel after regeneration.
Qe0 (mg/g) was equilibrium adsorption capacity of hydrogel.

JEOL JSM-6701FGS- M-5600LV scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JEOL, Japan) was used to get images. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a TF20 transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was measured on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). Model FTS-23000 (Digilab, America) spectrometer was used to characterize the prepared hydrogel to record the FTIR spectra. T6 New Century UV/vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi, China) and FluoroMax-4 Spectro Fluorometer (Horiba, USA) were used to record ultraviolet/visible (UV/vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra. The concentrations of heavy metals were detected by Agilent 725-ES ICP-OES (Agilent, USA).

Gravimetric analysis was used to examine the swelling ability of hydrogel samples. The prepared hydrogel sample was put into a constant temperature drying oven. After drying to a constant weight, the quality of the hydrogel was recorded. Then the hydrogel was immersed in deionized water until the swelling balance was balanced. The free water of the hydrogel was removed, and once again record the quality of the hydrogel at the moment. The equilibrium swelling ratio (SRt) of the hydrogel was calculated by Eq. (12).
(12)
S
R t
=
ω −
ω 0
ω 0
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of self-healing double network fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The steps of preparing double network self-healing BCS hydrogel were shown in Scheme 1 . The boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) were entrapped into the polyacrylamide (PAM) networks successfully, lots of -OH and –COOH groups on the surfaces of B-CDs inclined to form hydrogen-bond with the –C=O groups of the PAM polymer networks. The other network was prepared by the schiff base reaction between oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose and gelatin [31]. Furthermore, the imine bond contained in the structure was a dynamically reversible chemical bond, synthetic materials with this bond could be repaired or reshaped possibly. The structure not only increased the crosslinking density of the hydrogel network to improve mechanical properties, but also helped to form a dynamic crosslinking process and achieved self-healing. As a new promising nanomaterial, boron-doped carbon dots have high selectivity to heavy metals. The carbon dots embedded in the hydrogel made the hydrogel emit bright fluorescence, at the same time, they acted as a small molecule cross-linker between the polymers. As the crosslinking degree and the amount of hydrogen bonds increased, the mechanical strength of the composite hydrogel was improved. As a result, the hydrogels can be used as fluorescent sensor for detecting heavy metal ions, and adsorption materials for heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of self-healing double network fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were introduced into the hydrogel for the fluorescent detection of the heavy metal ions. UV–vis was recorded to reveal the fluorescent behavior of the synthesized B-CDs. Fig. 1a showed the spectrum of the B-CDs, which displayed a UV–visible broad shoulder at 350 nm and a strong fluorescence emission peak at 441 nm. Fig. 1b showed the morphology of the carbon dots, the diameter was approximately 5 nm and well dispersed. There were abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots, which was confirmed by FTIR characterization (Fig. 1c). The peak at 3427 cm−1 was attributed to the OH stretch vibration peak, the sharp peak at 1097 cm−1 was ascribed as BC bending vibration, and the absorption peak from asymmetric BO stretching at 1406 cm−1 [32].

In order to further conduct surface analysis, XPS was used to probe carbon dots. Fig. S1a showed the wide scan of B-CDs. Three peaks at 284.2, 285.9 and 288.3 eV (Fig. S1b) of the C1s spectrum represented the C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O and C=O/C=N groups, respectively [33]. In Fig. S1c, the O 1 s spectrum exhibited three fitted peaks at 531.8, 533.7, and 534.8 eV, which were associated with CO, C-OH, and C-O-C groups, respectively ([34]; Duet al, 2020). Besides, the high-resolution spectrum of B1s revealed two peaks at 190.9 and 194.5 eV, which were attributed to BC and BO groups (Fig. S1d), respectively [35,48]. The above results indicated that these carbon dots were oxygen-doped, boron-rich photoluminescent polymer carbon dots and there were many hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots.

Inspired by the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, we prepared BCS hydrogel with self-healing and comprehensive ability to detect and remove Ag+. Fig. 2a was the FTIR spectra of the self-healing BCS hydrogel. Oxi-CMC had a strong absorption peak at 3472 cm−1and 1737 cm−1, which attributed to the OH group and the aldehyde group (-HC=O) [28]. The AM monomer exhibited a characteristic peak of CH of the terminal double bond (-CH=CH2) at 964 cm−1 [36]. The characteristic bands at 1645, and1140 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching vibration and NH2 in-plane rocking, respectively. In the gelatin, the bands at 1675 cm−1, and 1535 cm−1were assigned to the absorption peaks of Amide I and Amide II, and the bands at 3305 cm−1was attributed to the absorption peaks of the amide group (NH) [37]. It was obvious that the FTIR spectrum of the self-healing BCS hydrogel showed that the stretching vibration peak of the aldehyde groups almost disappeared and a new imide peak (-CONHN=CH-) appeared at 1631 cm−1 (comparing with oxi-CMC), which demonstrated that the Schiff base reaction between oxi-CMC and gelatin occurred, the oxi-CMC/gelatin network had formed. The absence of the characteristic peak of acrylamide = C-H in the hydrogel indicated that the AM monomers were polymerized. The characteristic peaks at 1645, 1140 cm−1corresponding to CO stretching and NH2 in-plane rocking were shifted to 1631, and 1113 cm−1, proving the formation of hydrogen-bonding(C=O---H–N) between B-CDs and PAM in the self-healing BCS hydrogel [25–27,38]. It could be seen from the XPS spectrum of the self-healing BCS hydrogel, the three peaks of C 1 s 287.8, 285.8 and 284.4 eV were attributed to the CO, C-N/C-C and CC groups, respectively (Fig. 2b). The spectrum of N 1s exhibiting peak at 399.3 eV assigned to–NH2/-NH-, the groups could provide abundant active sites for adsorption of heavy metals (Fig. 2c). The O 1s at 531.5 eV could be attributed to the -COOH group, which also played a role in the excellent adsorption of heavy metal ions. Fig. S2 (a-b) showed the wide scan and B 1s spectrum of the hydrogel. The spectrum of B1s revealed two peaks at 190.9 and 197.2 eV that represented BC and BC2O/BCO2 groups respectively, which proved that the carbon dots were successfully fixed in the self-healing BSC hydrogel (Fig. S2b). In the SEM images (Fig. 2d), we noted that the morphology of the self-healing BCS hydrogel was a three-dimensional network structure with uniformly distributed pores, and the B-CDs were embedded in this network, it was essential for the adsorption and detection of Ag ions. In Fig. S3, it could be seen that the expansion rate of the hydrogel in the water gradually increased and reached equilibrium after about 23.3 h. The results showed that the self-healing BCS hydrogel had good swelling ability, high internal permeability, could achieve high-efficiency adsorption of heavy metals.

To evaluate the thermodynamic stability of the hydrogels, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on B-CDs, hydrogels without B-CDs, and self-healing BCS hydrogels (Fig. 3 ). The TGA spectrum of the carbon dots presented four stages of weightlessness. The initial weight loss was about 12.479 % at 25–230 °C, which was mainly due to the evaporation of water. The weight loss at 230–396 °C was 7.530 %, the weight loss between 396 and 545 °C continued to decrease to 9.180 %, which was mainly caused by the thermal decomposition of groups on the surface of B-CDs, the weight loss decreased between 545 and 800 °C possibly due to the release of pyrolysis gas. The TGA of the hydrogel without B-CDs mainly had four stages of weight loss, from room temperature to 180 °C, the mass loss was due to the evaporation of free and bound water in the hydrogel network. The weight loss from 180 °C to 330 °C was mainly caused by the breaking of the cross-linking bond between the oxidized carboxymethyl cellulose and the gelatin, the carbonization of the carboxyl groups on the molecular chain, the thermal decomposition of the gelatin and the amine groups on some PAM chains. The weight loss observed at 330–580 °C was mainly due to the further cracking of the PAM cross-linked nodes and the breaking of the chain skeleton. The decomposition in the range of 580–800 °C was mainly due to the carbonization of the skeleton carbon chain of the hydrogel to form small molecular substances. Compared with hydrogels without B-CDs, the low mass loss of self-healing BCS hydrogels was most likely due to the introduction of carbon dots, which increased the hydrogen bond content and the cross-linked structure in the hydrogel network, improved the hydrogel thermal stability of composite materials.

The fluorescence spectra was used to detect the self-healing BCS hydrogel. An excitation peak could be observed at 350 nm and an emission peak at 450 nm (Fig. 4a), demonstrating the feasibility of the hydrogel as fluorescent probes. Fig. 4b showed the fluorescence intensity of the self-healing BCS hydrogel after storage for 7 days. The results proved that the fluorescence intensity of the self-healing BCS hydrogel could remain unchanged for a long time and could be stored for a long time even without additional protection.

The capability of self-healing BCS hydrogel to detect silver metal ions was studied. At the onset of the experiment, it was a comparison of sensing characteristics of self-healing BCS hydrogel with 11 metal ions in aqueous solutions. As shown in Fig. 4c, Ag(I) ions caused obvious fluorescence quenching of self-healing BCS hydrogels, while other metal ions only slightly quenched. It showed that the prepared BCS hydrogel had good selectivity for the detection of Ag(I) ions. Meanwhile, 11 coexisting metal ions were added to the self-healing BCS hydrogel-Ag(I) system respectively to study its anti-interference ability, we noticed that the addition of coexisting metal ions did not cause a significant change in fluorescence quenching and showed no obvious quenching interference (Fig. 4d). It could be proved that Ag(I) ions were the most sensitive substance among these metal ions, and the self-healing BCS hydrogel had good selectivity for the detection of Ag(I) ions. This quenching effect could be ascribed to the hydroxyl groups on the boron doped carbon dots (B-CDs), which could directly reduce Ag+ to elemental silver (Ag0), thus growing metal nanoparticles on BCS hydrogel was feasible [39]. The fluorescence response of the self-healing BCS hydrogel to different heavy metal ions, such as Ag+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cr3+ under UV light was shown in Fig. S4. In the presence of Ag+, we could find the fast and total quenching, this phenomenon proved that the self-healing BCS hydrogel has a specific sensitivity toward Ag+ over other heavy metal ions.

In order to test the fluorescent sensitivity of the prepared self-healing BCS hydrogel to Ag+, the effect of different concentrations of Ag(I) ions on the fluorescence of the hydrogel was studied. Fig. 5a manifested the FL intensity of self-healing BCS hydrogel at 450 nm, which was decreased with the increase of the concentration (0–400 μM) of Ag+. It could be seen that FL intensity had a good linear relationship with the concentration of silver ions in the range of 0 μM–75 μM (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.798 μM and R2 = 0.9918. After being exposed to different concentrations of Ag(I), the fluorescence color of the self-healing BCS hydrogels changed from blue to black under UV light, and the fluorescence was completely quenched when the silver ion concentration was 400 mg/L (Fig. 5c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity to heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption process of heavy metal ions (Cu2+) to self-healing BCS hydrogel was also shown in Fig. 6a. The hydrogel was immersed in a beaker containing 100 mL of H2O and 100 mg of CuSO4·5H2O. After 1 h, the color of the blue solution became significantly lighter. This result confirmed that self-healing BCS hydrogel has excellent adsorption capacity for heavy metal ions. Accordingly, we carried out the Ag+ adsorption experiment in detail. The pH value played a key role that affects the adsorption of heavy metals by the self-healing BCS hydrogel. Therefore, we evaluated the effect of pH on the adsorption of Ag+ from the pH range of 1.0 to 7.0. The adsorption capacity of self-healing BCS hydrogel to Ag+ increased first and then decreased with the increase of pH, it reached the maximum at pH 5.0 (Fig. 6b). This was attributed to the fact that the adsorbent was protonated and positively charged at low pH, which inhibited the adsorption of silver ions. With the continuous increase of pH value, there was electrostatic interaction between the adsorbent and silver ions to increase the adsorption capacity. The further increase of pH would slightly decrease the adsorption capacity, which was caused by the precipitation of the hydrolysis of silver ions. The initial concentration on the adsorption of Ag(I) by self-healing BCS hydrogel was also studied. In Fig. 6c, the adsorption capacity of the self-healing BCS hydrogel increased with the increasing of the initial concentration of Ag+. Four adsorption models of the self-healing BCS hydrogel for Ag+ were shown in Fig. 6d and Fig. S5a-c. The relative linear fitting plots were given in Table. S1 and Table. S2, compared to the correlation coefficients (R2) of the Freundlich isotherm fitting curves, Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm model and Temkin isotherm model fitting curves, the adsorption of Ag+ onto the self-healing BCS hydrogel fitted the Langmuir model better, indicating that the process of Ag+ adsorption was monolayer adsorption. Based on the experiment results, the maximum adsorption capacity of the self-healing BCS hydrogel for Ag+ was calculated to be 407 mg/g, which was higher than those of the recently reported adsorbents (Table 1 ). Meanwhile, the adsorption capacity of the hydrogels for Cu2+ and Pb2+ was also studied. As shown in Fig. S5d, the maximum adsorption capacities for Pb2+ and Cu2+ reached 1250, and 1111 mg/g, respectively. The results confirmed that the self-healing BCS hydrogel has excellent adsorption capacity for heavy metal ions.

To explore the adsorption mechanism of Ag+ to self-healing BCS hydrogels, we studied the influence of contact time on Ag+ adsorption capacity. As shown in Fig. 6e, the self-healing BCS hydrogel adsorbed Ag+ rapidly at first, and subsequently the adsorption speed slowed down until it reached equilibrium after about 50 min. The linear fitting results of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and intra-particle diffusion model were shown in Fig. S6a, Fig. 6f and Fig. S6b, respectively. The kinetic parameters of Ag+ were shown in Table. S3, obviously, the correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9978) was closer to 1, which could better describe the adsorption process of Ag+ to the self-healing BCS hydrogel. The applicability of second order kinetics to the current data suggested that the determination adsorption process for Ag+ was chemical adsorption. Fig. S6b presented that the intra-particle diffusion plot did not pass through the origin, which illustrated that adsorption process involved intra-particle diffusion, but the intra-particle diffusion was not the solely rate-limiting step. At the same time, the fitting curve of the intra-particle diffusion presented two-stage linear portions, which implied that the adsorption process was managed by the two adsorption stages. The intra-particle diffusion rate constants in Table S4 followed the order of kp,1 > kp,2. The first linear portion represented the external surface adsorption or instantaneous diffusion stage. At this stage, large amounts of Ag+ were rapidly adsorbed by the exterior surface of hydrogel groups before gradually diffused into the inside of the hydrogel, the adsorption was close to equilibrium in the second stages finally.

The properties of the adsorbents such as easy regeneration, reusability, and high adsorption capacity made them very useful in industrial and large-scale applications. Fig. 7 showed the adsorption-desorption cycles of copper and lead ions by self-healing BCS hydrogels. The desorbed hydrogel was reused for the next adsorption process. In this study, 0.1 M HCl solution was used as the desorbent for heavy metal ions. The experimental results showed that the maximum amount of Cu2+ adsorbed by the hydrogel was 86.59 % in the first cycle and 74.60 % in the last cycle (after seven cycles). The maximum adsorption capacity of Pb2+ by the hydrogel composite was 87.13 % in the first cycle and 80.98 % in the last cycle. The results of cyclic regeneration proved that the hydrogel could maintain a good adsorption capacity for Cu2+ and Pb2+ even after seven consecutive recycling cycles, which indicated that the self-healing BCS hydrogel has good reusability. It could be used as a green, environmentally friendly and high-performance adsorbent for practical wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of adsorption and fluorescence sensing of heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption and detection mechanism of self-healing BCS hydrogels were analyzed using FTIR and XPS method. Fig. 8a showed the FTIR spectra, the self-healing BCS hydrogel had peaks at 3422, 1642 and 1450 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH, CO, and CN, respectively. For the self-healing BCS hydrogel-Ag+, the peak of CO shifted to 1630 cm−1, and all peaks' intensity was reduced. These changes indicated that -OH, -NH2 and –COOH groups and Ag(I) ions form complexes through strong chelation. It was also proved by the XPS analysis in Fig. 8b, a new peak at 372.2ev appeared after Ag+ adsorption. The peak was assigned to Ag 3d, indicating that Ag ions were adsorbed on BCS hydrogel. The high-resolution spectrum of Ag 3d of the self-healing BCS hydrogel-Ag+ exhibited two peaks at the binding energies of 368 and 374 eV, which were assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2. The results well indicated silver ions were partially reduced to metallic silver (Fig. 8c). A new binding energy peak at 532.9 eV appeared after reduction (Fig. 8d), the reason could be attribute to the oxidization of –C-OH groups to –C=O groups of B-CDs in the hydrogel, indicating the structure change of self-healing BCS hydrogel. In the spectrum of N 1s, an increase in the intensity of the characteristic peak of -NH2 was observed after adsorption, indicating that the amino group was involved in the adsorption (Fig. 8e).

Based on the above discussion, the mechanism of adsorption and fluorescence sensing of heavy metal ions was illustrated in Fig. 9 When the self-healing BCS hydrogel was immersed in the ionic solution, the metal ions were captured by the hydrogel by adsorption. The three-dimensional network structure of the self-healing BCS hydrogel gave ion transmission channels, the Ag+ diffused from the surface to the interior through these channels. Furthermore, there were lots of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups in the hydrogel, which provided abundant adsorption sites to accumulate Ag+ through chelation/electrostatic mutual attraction. When Ag+ were captured by the hydrogel, self-healing BCS hydrogel were fluorescent turn-off. On the one hand, there was a static quenching effect. The interaction between -OH, -COOH groups of self-healing BCS hydrogel and Ag+ formed non-fluorescent ground state complexes. On the other hand, there was a light-induced electron transfer effect. The prepared self-healing BCS hydrogel could be used as an electron donor, and could transfer electrons to metal ions, the electrons were combined with Ag+, and Ag+ was reduced to Ag0, thereby inhibiting the radiated electron-hole recombination on the surface of the carbon dots, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence emission. The carbon dots in the hydrogel reduced the silver ions to silver, and the silver was enriched in the hydrogel, the fluorescence of the hydrogel was quenched, which could quickly detect and adsorb silver ions. In addition, there was no obvious overlap between the fluorescence excitation/emission peaks of B-CDs and the ultraviolet-visible absorption peaks of Ag+, which proved there is no fluorescence resonance energy transfer and internal filtering effects (Fig. S6) [47].

Fig. 10 was graph of self-healing BCS hydrogel self-healed at room temperature for 4 h. As shown in the Fig. 10a, the hydrogel was first cut into two halves, then the two halves of the hydrogel were brought into contact with each other, after a while, the hydrogel could be stretched. As shown in Fig. 10b, the hydrogel was cut into two halves, one half adsorbed silver ions, another half did not adsorb any ions, and the two halves of the hydrogel were brought into contact with each other. After 4 h, the hydrogel self-healed, and the self-healed hydrogel could be stretched. The reason was due to the dynamic Schiff base reaction and hydrogen bonds between the hydrogel networks. The self-healing mechanism of the self-healing BCS hydrogel was shown in the Fig. 10c.

In summary, we have prepared a new type of self-healing fluorescent BCS hydrogel with efficient adsorption and sensitive detection of silver ions. The maximum adsorption capacity of the hydrogel for silver ions was as high as 407 mg/g, a linear fluorescence response range for Ag+(0-75 μM) with a detection limit of 3.798 μM. Based on reversible dynamic crosslinking and hydrogen bonding, the hydrogel have excellent self-healing properties, it could be easily regenerated 7 times without loss of adsorption performance. This study showed that self-healing hydrogels could simultaneously detect and adsorb heavy metal ions, have great practical application potential in the adsorption and removal of heavy metal ions in sewage.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Funds for Creative Research Groups of Gansu Province (No. 21JR7RA160), the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22174110, 22127803), the Industrial Support Plan of Gansu Provincial Department of Education (2021cyzc-01) and Special Fund Project for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development by the Central Government (No. 2020-2060503-17).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2022.06.029.
